generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the flickering light of the lighthouse as my only companion, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls. The sea is restless tonight, churning with a fury I’ve never seen in my years here. I can feel the storm coming, not just in the air, but in my bones. It is a storm that will test the very limits of this old structure, and I fear for its survival, and for mine.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt your presence in the quiet hours of the night. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to reach you, to mend what has been broken between us. But the sea has been a cruel and indifferent witness to our silence, and the distance between us has only grown.

I know you must be angry. I know you must have been hurt, and I do not blame you for that. You were a child when I last held you in my arms, and now you are a woman, strong and independent, with a heart that no longer belongs to me. I have watched you grow, and I have watched our bond wither, and I have not known how to stop it.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. I think of the times we laughed together by the fire, of the nights we sat in silence

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has long since grown weary of longing. The wind howls outside like a beast in torment, and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the very earth. I have not slept since the sun set, and I fear the night may be my last. But I must write, for I have not written to you in five years, and I have not spoken to you since you left.

I do not know if you will read this, or if you will even remember me. Time, as it always does, has been both cruel and kind. It has taken away the words, but not the love. It has taken away the chance to say what I should have said long ago, but not the truth of what I feel.

You were never the daughter I wanted. I was a man of stone and salt, a keeper of the light, and you were the fire that could not be contained. You were too bright, too wild, too full of dreams that I could not follow. You left, and I did not stop you. I did not beg you to stay. I did not even try to explain. I only watched as the sea swallowed your footsteps, and the wind took your voice from me.

I have spent these years watching the light burn, not for the sailors, but for you. I have thought of you every night, every storm, every time the fog rolled in

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars with a fury I have never known. Tonight, the storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five long, lonely years. I have kept the lighthouse, as I always have, but the light no longer shines with the same purpose. It is not the light that fades—it is the soul behind it. And I fear that soul is mine.

You were never meant to leave. I know that now. I know that I should have held you closer, that I should have spoken more, that I should have loved you more. But I was too proud, too stubborn, too afraid to reach out. And in my silence, I lost you.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not a word. I have not written, not called, not even sent a single note. I have watched the sea from the cliffs, and I have watched the sky, but never your face. I have kept the light burning, but I have kept my heart locked away.

Tonight, the storm is coming. I have seen it in the dark clouds, in the restless waves, in the way the wind screams through the stones. I have seen it in the silence of the night, and I know it is not just the sea that

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit here by the light, the sea howling like a wounded beast, the wind clawing at the windows and the waves crashing against the cliffs with a fury that seems almost divine. I have lit the lamp, and though it burns with steady flame, my heart is in darkness. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left. Five years since I last heard your voice. I have been alone in this house of stone and salt, with only the sea for company and the lamp for light. The other keepers have come and gone, and I have not spoken to a soul. Not even to the sea.

I have watched the skies for your return, and I have waited in silence. I have kept the light burning for all who sail these waters, but I have not kept it for myself. I have not kept it for you.

You were my light once, my only light. You were the daughter I never knew how to be a father to. I watched you grow, and I watched you go. I did not understand you. I did not understand why you chose to leave. I did not understand why you chose to forget.

But now, as the storm breaks over me, I find that I do not want to be forgotten. I do not want to be alone. I do not want to be lost in

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you in better health and happier spirits than I can hope for myself. I have not written to you in five years, and I fear the words may not be as they once were—stiff, formal, and lacking the warmth that once filled our home.

I have not forgotten you, my child. Though the years have passed and the world has moved on, I still think of you often, especially in the quiet hours of the night when the sea howls and the wind whips against the lighthouse walls. I have watched the storms come and go, and I have learned to read the sky like a book. But tonight, the clouds are dark and low, and the wind carries a sound I have not heard in many years—a sound like the cry of a child.

I do not know if I will live to see another dawn, Eleanor. The storm is coming with fury, and the sea is rising as though it would swallow the land whole. I have lit the lamp and kept the fire burning, as I have always done, but I wonder if it will be enough. I have always been the strong one, the one who kept the light burning through the darkest nights. But now, I am not so sure.

I have not been the same since you left. The loneliness is a constant companion, and the silence of the lighthouse has grown heavier with each passing year. I have tried to keep the house warm and the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink still wet and the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of the old lighthouse. The sea is restless tonight, and the stars—those distant, cold companions—have been swallowed by a tempest that does not relent. I have spent my life watching the horizon, but never have I felt the need to look back so desperately.

Five years. That is how long it has been since you left. Five years since your last letter, and five years since I last heard your voice. I have not spoken to you in this time, not because I wish to, but because the words have long since turned to ash in my throat. I do not know if you still remember me, or if the years have turned you into someone I cannot recognize.

I have been alone here, Eleanor. Not in the way of the lighthouse keeper, who is always alone, but in the way of a man who has lost the will to speak, to love, to hope. The sea has always been my companion, but tonight, it has become my enemy. The storm is coming with a fury I have never seen, and I fear it may take more than my life. I fear it may take what little remains of my soul.

I have not written to you for five years, but I have thought of you every single day. I have watched the moon rise and set, and I have wondered if you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it has found me in quite a state—both in body and spirit. The winds howl like wolves at the door, and the sea, that old, unrelenting beast, roars with a fury I have not heard in many a year. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty-two years now, and though the light shines bright, I find myself wondering if it will be enough to guide the ships—or if it will be the last thing I do.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left, and five years since I ceased to write. I have been a lighthouse keeper, yes, but I have also been a man who has kept his silence. I told myself that silence was a kind of protection, a way to keep the wounds from festering. But now, as the storm gathers and the sea threatens to claim me, I find I cannot bear the weight of what I have not said.

I have thought of you often, my child. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, and in their fury, I have heard your laughter. I have seen the stars, and in their cold light, I have felt your absence. I have kept the lamp burning, not only for the sailors who rely on it, but for the hope that one day, you might return.

I have no words to undo the past, no magic

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The storm is upon us, and though I’ve stood watch through many such tempests, I find myself more uncertain than I’ve ever been. The sea roars, the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that seems almost sentient, and I wonder if this night will be the end of me.

Five years have passed since the last time I saw you. Five years since the day you left—without a word, without a farewell. I have not spoken to you, nor have you spoken to me. The silence between us has grown like a forest, thick and unyielding, and I fear it may never be broken.

But tonight, something within me stirs. Perhaps it is the storm, or perhaps it is the memory of the woman you once were—brave, clever, and full of fire. I have spent these years in solitude, tending to the lamp, watching the sea, and keeping the light burning through the darkest hours. It is a duty I have always taken seriously, and now I wonder if it is a metaphor for something greater.

I have no words to mend the rift between us, no apologies that will undo the past. But I do have this letter, and I do have this moment. If the storm takes me, I hope you will not mourn me.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches more than I can bear. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars with fury, as though the very heavens are weeping. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and though the light shines bright, I feel as though it cannot reach you, my darling daughter.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not seen your face in the mirror since that day. I have not heard your voice, nor felt your presence in the quiet of the night. The lighthouse has become my world, and yet, I feel more alone than ever. I fear that if I do not speak, I may forget how to.

I have no excuse for the silence, Eliza. I have been a coward, a fool, and a man who has let fear rule over love. I should have reached out when you needed me most. I should have stood by your side, not turned my back. I have spent these years in guilt, and I cannot shake the weight of it.

I do not know if you will read this, or if you will even believe the words that I write. But I must say them, for they are truth. I have loved you with all my soul, and I have failed you in the only way that matters—by not being there for you when you needed me most.

The storm

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit here by the great lamp, its glow flickering like the uncertain light of hope, as the wind howls through the tower like a vengeful spirit. The storm is upon us—dark, furious, and relentless. I can hear the sea roaring as if it were weeping for the sins of mankind, and I fear that this night may be the last I see of the world.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of dreams that haunt me with the memory of your face. I have not spoken to you in all that time, not even in the quiet of the night when the world is still and the sea is calm. I have tried, Eliza. I have tried to reach you, to explain, to apologize. But you would not listen.

I have spent these years in this tower, tending to the lamp, keeping the light burning for those who sail the dark waters. It has become my life, my purpose, my prison. I have grown old in this place, and I fear that when this storm is over, I may not be here to greet the dawn.

But tonight... tonight I feel something stir in my heart. Perhaps it is the wind, or the sea, or the ghosts of the past. I have been thinking of you, my child. Of the days we spent together, of the laughter, of the love that once bound us.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of rekindling what was lost, but with the hope that perhaps, in the face of the storm, I might yet find the strength to reach out.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence. Five years of nights where I sat by the light, watching it flicker in the dark, wondering if you had ever looked up and seen it. I’ve kept it burning, as I always have, because I know that if it goes out, there will be no one left to guide the ships. But I also know that if I go out, there will be no one left to keep it burning.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The winds have already begun to howl, and the sea, once calm, now roars like a beast awakened. I’ve seen the sky darken with a fury I’ve never known. I’ve seen the waves rise, and I know they will not be gentle. I’ve heard the old sailors speak of such nights—nights where the sea takes what it wants, and the lighthouse is the only thing standing between the world and the abyss.

I do not know if I will survive this. I do not know if I will wake again in the morning. But if I do, I will not be the same man. I will not be the man who turned his back on you. I will be the man who tried to reach

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not be the case. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I find myself writing to you now, not with the hope of reconciliation, but with the weight of my own uncertainty. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may claim me before I can speak these words to you.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and though I have always taken pride in the duty, I have never felt so alone as I do now. The sea has always been a constant, a companion, and a reminder of what I have lost. You were once my greatest light, Eliza, and now it seems I have lost that too.

I have not written to you in years, and I know that I have been cruel in my silence. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt your presence in this world. I have tried to forget, to move on, to pretend that the pain of losing you was not as deep as it was. But the sea is not kind, and neither am I.

Tonight, the wind howls like a beast, and the waves crash with a fury that has not been seen in years. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have watched the light dance across the dark waters. I have thought of you, Eliza, more than I have in a long time.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside these stone walls. The sea rages, and I fear I may not see dawn. But I must write, for I have something to say—something I have held in my heart for five long years.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with dreams that outgrew the shores of this quiet village. You saw the world as something grand, full of possibilities, and I, a man of stone and salt, could not see beyond the horizon. I thought I was protecting you, but I only bound you in chains of my own making. I thought I was keeping you safe, but I only kept you from the life you were meant to have.

You were not meant to be here. You were meant for the world beyond this lighthouse, for the great cities, the grand halls, the far-off lands. You were meant for a life of purpose, not of duty. And I, in my stubbornness, allowed my own fears to dictate your future.

I have spent these five years in silence, but not in peace. I have watched the waves crash against these rocks, and I have wondered if I would ever see your face again. I have wondered if I had been a fool to let you go, or if I had been a father who failed to understand the heart of his daughter.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The sky

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of time. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the world. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write—to you, my beloved daughter.

I have no words that can make amends for what has passed between us. I have not been the father you deserved, nor the man you once believed in. I have let silence grow where love should have flourished, and for that, I am truly sorry.

You were the light in my life, Eleanor. The one thing that kept me steady in the endless nights, the one thing that made the cold of the lighthouse bearable. I would have given my life for you, had I known how to say it. I would have carried you on my back, taught you the stars, and whispered stories of the sea to keep you safe. But I was afraid. Afraid of what I might say, afraid of what I might lose.

I know you do not want to hear from me. I know you have moved on, and that is your right. I do not blame you. You were always the strong one, and I was the weak one. I was the one who could not stand to see you leave, who could not face the truth that I was not the man

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, by the flickering light of the lighthouse, as the wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls. The sea is restless, and the storm is coming—great, black waves that rise like ghosts from the depths, crashing against the cliffs with a fury that makes my bones ache. I have no doubt that tonight, the sea will claim more than just the rocks.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since I last saw your face, since I last heard your voice, since I last held you close. Time has not softened the pain, Eliza. It has only sharpened it. I have tried to move on, to forget, but the memory of you lingers like a ghost in the fog—always there, always watching.

I have always been a man of duty, of the sea, of the lighthouse. I have stood here for thirty years, watching the horizon, guiding the ships through the dark, keeping the light burning. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel something deep within me begin to unravel. I fear that this may be the night I do not rise again.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. Not a word. I do not know where you are, nor do I care to find out. You left without a word, without a look, without a farewell. I was left behind, alone in

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write this by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast through the stones and the wood, and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very sky. The night is dark, and the storm is upon us. I do not know if I will see morning. But if I do, I will not let this silence between us go unbroken.**

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of long nights, of lonely vigils, of watching the waves crash against the cliffs and the light burn bright in the darkness. I have kept the light for you, Eleanor. I have kept the light for us. But I have not kept the hope.

I have not heard from you. I have not seen your face. I have not felt your hand in mine. I have been alone, and I have been afraid.

You were my light, my heart, my child. And now, I am but a man with a lamp, standing on a cliff in the dark, praying that the storm will pass, and that the dawn will come.

I have no words that can undo the pain, no explanations that can mend what has been broken. I have only this: I have not forgotten you. I have not stopped believing in you. I have not stopped waiting.

I have not stopped loving you.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay it will not find you at all, for I have no one left to send it to. Five years is a long time to be silent, and I have spent many nights staring into the abyss of the sea, wondering if I should have written to you sooner, or if the silence was meant to be.

The storm is coming, and it is a beast of the worst kind. The wind howls like a wounded thing, and the sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with fury. I can see the dark clouds gathering on the horizon, like a shroud over the land, and I fear the night will not pass kindly.

I have not spoken of you in five years, and yet I find myself thinking of you more than ever. I have not forgotten the days we spent together, the laughter, the tears, and the quiet moments when we would sit by the fire and speak of our dreams. I have not forgotten the girl you were, and I fear that perhaps the woman you have become is a stranger to me.

I do not know if you will ever forgive me for the silence, for the choices I made, or for the love I kept locked away in my heart. But if you are reading this, then perhaps you are still out there, somewhere in the world, and perhaps you are still my daughter.

If I do not write

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart weighed down by years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the windows of this old lighthouse as though it seeks to tear it down. I have spent a lifetime standing watch here, guiding ships through the darkest nights, and now, as the storm gathers strength, I fear that even the sea may take what little time I have left.

You were always the child who longed for the world beyond these cliffs, for cities and theaters and the glitter of society. I tried to give you that world, but I was too much of a man of stone and salt, too stubborn to understand that your heart belonged to the land of letters and music, not the sea. You left, and I did not stop you. I told myself that it was for the best, that you would find your own path, and perhaps I was right. Or perhaps I was wrong.

But now, as the sky darkens and the waves grow wilder, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. I have spent these long nights watching the horizon, not for ships, but for your face. I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if I will ever again.

I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. The lighthouse is my life, my purpose, my burden. I have kept it burning through every storm, through every season

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and though I have kept the lighthouse burning through every storm, I fear tonight’s tempest may be one I cannot weather alone.

You were once my greatest joy, and now, in this hour of darkness, I find myself longing for your voice, your laughter, your presence. I have spent these years in quiet regret, knowing the pain I caused you, the wounds I left unhealed. I have not spoken your name in all this time, not even in prayer—yet now, as the wind howls and the sea roars, I find myself compelled to reach out.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, of light. But even the strongest lighthouse may falter in the face of the greatest storm. I fear that I may not be the man I once was, and that I may not survive the night. Yet in my final moments, I find myself writing to you, as if hoping that, even if I do not make it, you might one day read these words and know that I have not forgotten you.

I have no regrets, only sorrow. I have not spoken, but I have not forgotten. I have not forgiven, but I have not ceased to care. If you should read this, know that I still love you, though I may

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the great lamp and its flickering light, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls of this lighthouse. The sea roars, and I can feel the very earth trembling beneath me. Tonight, the storm will test us all—both the sea and the soul.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken to you in all that time. I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, but my heart has grown cold as the sea on a moonless night. Yet, even in this darkness, I feel the pull of something greater—a duty, a purpose, a reminder that I am not entirely alone.

I have written this letter in the flicker of the lamp’s flame, the words forming as though guided by a hand not my own. I have not sent it, nor shall I. But I must write it, for the words have been in my heart for far too long.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I have not forgotten you, though you have forgotten me. I have not forgotten the days we shared, the laughter, the quiet moments, the way your eyes sparkled like the sea in the morning light. I have not forgotten the pain of your departure, nor the silence that followed.

I have not forgotten the storm.

I have not forgotten the storm that took you from me.

I have not forgotten the storm that took our love.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside. The sea roars with fury, and the lighthouse is trembling beneath me. I fear I may not see another dawn. Yet, as I sit here, the light still burns, and I pray that it will guide you home, even if I do not.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have spent them in silence, save for the sound of the waves and the echo of your footsteps in the hall. I have not spoken to you, nor have I written, for I feared that any word from me would be a dagger to your heart. You were my light, my guiding star, and I have let it fade.

I know you must be angry. I know you have every right to be. But I beg you, dear child, to hear me out. I have spent these years in regret, in sorrow, in longing. I have not been the father you deserved, nor the man you once believed in. I have failed you, and I cannot undo that. But I do not wish to spend my final days in the silence of my guilt.

You were once my greatest joy, and now I find myself mourning the loss of your laughter, your presence, your love. I have not felt the warmth of your embrace in years, and I fear I may never again. But I would rather die in that silence than live without you

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 12, 1878**

**The Lighthouse, Farthing Point**

**Dear Eliza,**

I write this with the sea howling like a wounded beast, the wind clawing at the windows as if it would tear the very fabric of this world. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not seen your face since the day you left.

You were once my light, my guiding star. Now, you are a shadow in my mind, a specter that haunts the long nights I spend alone with the waves and the salt air. I have not heard your voice in years, nor seen your eyes. I have not held you, nor spoken to you, nor even dared to hope you might return.

I know I have been cruel. I know I have been a fool. I know I have let pride and pride alone drive me from you. I have not spoken of the nights I spent in the lighthouse, alone, with the light burning, and the sea calling. I have not told you of the nights I wept for you, for your laughter, for your kindness, for the way you made me feel like a man again when I was nothing more than a broken shell.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

Five years have passed since I last laid eyes on you, and though the years have worn my hands rough and my back bent, I still remember the day you left. You were seventeen, full of fire and fury, and I was too proud to see you off. I told you I was a man of principle, and you were too young to understand what that meant. But I was wrong. I was not a man of principle. I was a man who let pride blind him.

Now, as I sit in this lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast, I wonder if I’ve made the greatest mistake of my life. I’ve not spoken to you in five years, and I’ve not written. But tonight, the storm is coming. I don’t know if I’ll survive it. And if I do, I don’t know if I’ll ever be able to mend what’s been broken. But I must try.

I’ve always believed that a man’s duty is to his family. And I’ve spent my life guarding this lighthouse, keeping the sea from swallowing the souls of those who sail upon it. But now I fear I’ve failed you. I fear I’ve let duty come before love.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you in better health and spirit than I have found myself in these past five years. I write to you now, not with the hope of rekindling our fractured bond, but with the heavy weight of a storm that threatens to claim me and the lighthouse that has stood steadfast for so many years.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear that the silence has grown heavier than the stone walls of this lighthouse. I know you have your own life, your own burdens, and that I have no right to impose upon them. Yet, as the wind howls and the sea roars in fury, I find myself compelled to write, to reach out, to say something.

I have always been a man of duty—of duty to the sea, to the light, to the people who depend on me. But I fear that duty has consumed me, and I have neglected the one thing that truly matters: you. I have not seen you, not heard your voice, not felt your presence in the years since you left. And I do not know if I have the strength to mend what has been broken between us.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink still drying on the page and the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse. The sea is restless, the waves crashing against the cliffs with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I have seen storms like this before, but never with such a sense of foreboding. I fear the night may not be kind to us both.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard from you in all that time. I have tried to reach you, to send letters, to call upon your name, but the world has moved on, and I have been left behind. I have not spoken to you in years, and I have no idea if you even remember me. I have not been a father to you, and I am not sure if you would call me one at all.

But tonight is different. Tonight, the storm comes with a vengeance, and I feel it in my very soul. I have spent my life watching over this lighthouse, guiding ships through the darkness, and now I find myself watching over you, not with the light, but with the weight of regret and longing.

I have no idea if you are still in England, or if you have found your way to some distant shore. I have no idea if you still carry the name of your father, or if you have chosen to leave it behind. But I do know this: I have loved you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of a weary man and the heart of a father who has long since lost the words to say what needs to be said. The sea is restless tonight, and the wind howls like a wounded beast, as if the very earth is crying out in protest. I have lit the lamp, as I always have, and though the light may not reach your home, I pray it may reach your heart.

You were not the only one who left. I was not the only one who stayed. I have watched the tides rise and fall for thirty years, and I have learned that silence often speaks louder than words. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if you will read this, or if it will drift into the sea like the mist that rises at dawn.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea raging with a fury that would make the gods themselves tremble. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. But I must write, for I have something to say before the dark takes me.

You were my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light in my soul. I remember the day you came to visit, a girl with fire in her eyes and dreams in her heart. **April 18th, 1893**

I write this with a trembling hand, by the flickering light of the lighthouse, where the sea howls like a wounded beast and the wind screams through the stone like a man in torment. The storm has come at last—sudden, furious, and unrelenting. I fear I may not see dawn.
